summary:
The Jemdet Nasr period, spanning approximately 3100 to 3000 BCE, was a brief but distinctive phase of Mesopotamian prehistory that bridged the end of the [[event:1617|Uruk Period]] and the beginning of the Early Dynastic era. Named after the site of Jemdet Nasr in central Mesopotamia where its characteristic polychrome pottery was first identified, this period represents a phase of regional reorganization following the collapse of Uruk's long-distance exchange network, as Mesopotamian cities adjusted to more locally self-sufficient economic arrangements.

The Jemdet Nasr period is characterized by a distinctive ceramic style featuring polychrome painted decoration in red and black on a buff background, and by the continuation and further development of proto-cuneiform writing. Tablets from the Jemdet Nasr site itself reveal a distinctive administrative system using collective city-seals — combining the sign-designations of multiple cities on single tablets — suggesting either a confederation of cities sharing administrative functions or a standardized inter-city accounting system. This represents one of the earliest examples of inter-urban political or economic coordination in the historical record.

Metallurgy advanced during the Jemdet Nasr period, with bronze objects becoming more common as the [[event:44|Bronze Age]] technology spread across the region. The period also sees refinements in [[event:1621|cylinder seal]] iconography and the development of more complex narrative scenes. Although brief in duration, the Jemdet Nasr period was the immediate precursor to the Early Dynastic period when the full flowering of Sumerian city-state civilization — with its royal dynasties, monumental temples, and extensive literary tradition — would occur.
